item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What if...
This is a wild guess, and probably isn't even intended by Scantron :P

Eric Zann is the Eric, but just grown up.

item.title: Re: little note here
item.content:encoded:
EDIT: oops, sorry about the necro. I linked here from a current posting for the MTF2016 contest, and forgot I was no longer on a recent post.

Neurotypical isn't a real word. {…} So you may want to replace it with something else.

The OED, Q.E.D.:

neurotypical, adj. and n.

Pronunciation: Brit. neurotypical#_gb_2.mp3 /ˌnjʊərəʊˈtɪpᵻkl/ , U.S. neurotypical#_us_3.mp3 /ˌn(j)uroʊˈtɪpᵻk(ə)l/ , neurotypical#_us_2.mp3 /ˌn(j)ərəˈtɪpᵻk(ə)l/
Frequency (in current use): (image with two out of eight circles colored in)

Etymology: Formed within English, by compounding. Etymons: neuro- comb. form, typical adj.
< neuro- comb. form + typical adj.

A. adj.
Neurologically typical; spec. exhibiting ordinary, as opposed to autistic, thinking and behaviour.

B. n.
A neurotypical person.

Those darn lexicographers at the Oxford English Dictionary and their made up words ;^)

(Though in my experience, the word is also used in contrast clinical anxiety, clinical depression, ADD, and so on, not just autism. )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had a lot of clever things to say, but I am very tired, so I'll just leave it at; this is lovely and weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They lead up to me poking at people's irrational attachment to this arbitrary classification system. I care far more about discussion posts than rating, so if I don't feel that some part of a piece has any real connection to literary merit, I'll just do whatever gets people to make posts about it, even if it's not people's favorite thing.

So far, it's worked brilliantly :^)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yet another of the wierd attachments to the safe rating. these had better eventually lead up to something or i'm going back and downvoting every one. As for this one specifically it brings no great feelings either way. I had actually read the lovecraft story mentioned, and this doesnt connect enough for me.

item.title: Re: little note here
item.content:encoded:
From Wikipedia (you can check the citations there if you really want):

The concept was later adopted by both the neurodiversity movement and the scientific community.

Also lol @ "not being a real word" because it was made by a specific subculture to express a concept that didn't already have a word.

Regardless, the word is the most immediately understandable way to express the concept. The article refers to people with typical neurology. Neurotypical. Boom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: considering it'd be impossible to store every variant of even a relatively short song encoded by sine waves of identical length in all the matter of the universe, it is actually infinite because there'd be no way to tell whether or not we have heard a specific song before!

item.title: little note here
item.content:encoded: Neurotypical isn't a real word. It's a slang term coined by the autistic community. Using it is tantamount to using the word "terraform" in an article. So you may want to replace it with something else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: noisecoresteboygroup-Remixed by jb and heino.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The way this skip strikes me is basically, "In the future, there's dubstep, BUT WORSE!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one doesn't do much for me. The SCP itself isn't really that exciting on its own and the implications it's supposed to have seem forced on the reader. Zahn throws out the insistence that it predicts the future out of the blue in the first letter then the rest of the article treats it as fact without any indication there's anything to back it up. I'm left so skeptical and unimpressed by Zahn's assertions that the last letter loses all of its weight.

In my opinion the best SCPs give you reasons to be scared of them, while this one simply goes "trust me it's creepy". Having a narrative direct the horror rather than the item itself isn't necessarily a bad thing of course but in this instance I can't find a reason to trust Zahn or buy into the narrative because there's little else there.

Maybe if there was more to the SCP itself or maybe if there was some information on the Foundation's own tests on the records to give Zahn's fearmongering some weight I'd like it better, but as it stands I'm gonna have to -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, he peeked into the void of the future, and we're doomed. Big surprise. Where's the catch? The only thing that really interested me was the fact that the Foundation knew the composer. What's new here? What's unexpected? I'm tempted to downvote, but shall leave it at a neutral for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn, I liked this one. I don't think I've ever seen another SCP include the idea of someone willingly contacting the Foundation. It also just goes to show how something doesn't have to be some hideous eldritch creature from another universe in order to be scary. He's discovered…a glitch in the universe. or at least our understanding of it. And the one organization that actually cares won't even talk to him.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The issue I have with this SCP article is that there is very little attention given to the SCP itself. All the power has been given to the letters accompanying it. While they letters provide a direction for the Foundation to go into, they never do. A testing log, attempts to discover the method with which they were produced, listing of other anomalous property's that the Mr. Zann was unaware of. Any of these, or entries of a similar nature could expand and improve this article. The way it is written now however, it just doesn't flow strongly enough for me to up-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, long story short, the first tape sent by this guy showed that humanity is not as free as they think (if you can predict the music before it happens, it means we are under an influence, that is; somehow, controlling our fate). The second tape was a way to prove his theory, and the third one was when this guy predicted the future.

Oh and taking this spot to answer Deacon: He can not predict the future, but the future music tendency. It means his way to predict is actually really vague and directed to human collective music, not something as specific like Foundation' containment breachs! Unless there were a skip that need to be1contained with the power of music!

Footnotes
1. That actually doesn't sound so bad…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand what it actually does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Boring article, and tries way too hard to convince the reader that it's scarier and more interesting than it actually is. (Especially considering that we already have tons of much better articles that involve predicting the future.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Me too. Music is Shrek.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, I find your option far more scary than the end of the world!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Potentially. Or, we just lose ability or reason to make music.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems to me that after that third note, it should become a fairly high priority for the Foundation to locate and bring in this "Erich" fellow. If he can predict futures, he might be able to help stave off containment breaches, just as an example…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A weird question came to my mind here: what if at some point, this guy finds a dead end in predicting the music? I mean, if history and music are already predetermined, a dead end would mean the end of humanity?

item.title: Re: Minor Error
item.content:encoded: Sure thing. Good catch, thanks.

item.title: Minor Error
item.content:encoded: Can you throw an I after "believe" in "I believe have successfully predicted the future…" please?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was iffy on the "left at the Foundation's doorstep" deal, but the story behind this is good enough for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really quite clever and well written. I can't put a definite point to it, but it just feels right for an SCP document, accounting for the footnotes and everything. The only issue i have is the same one mole mentioned, we need some hint at the end as to what Erich has found. Not much, just some hint as to what freaked him out. That would wrap this scp up beautifully.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And nothing says it can. We never get even a glimpse or hint of what Zann actually found out. This guy uses a Lovecraft character as his pseudonym and sends rambling notes to secret corporations(that happen to exist, but that's another matter). He is probably not very sound of his mind, you know.

As I said, this needs more meat. Just a little bit of show, but in the end what we have is Lovecraftian melodrama.

(Don't get me wrong, I love Lovecraft, but his narration sure was purple and dramatic by the buckets)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't like it, and downvoted accordingly. IF he had figured out how to do this with things other than music, than his horror would be founded. But the ending really screwed it all up for me. There's no reason to be so fearful of just predetermining some musical pieces. That doesn't prove there's no free will, it just proves that there is a predictive pattern to SOME popular music timelines.

Now, if you wanted to make this SCP much more amusing, add a -J and change the blacked out name to Justin Beiber and suddenly the article, the tone, the implication, the horror would all be perfect and hilarious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think you read it thoroughly enough. We were able to confirm lyrics that an artist had not written yet. That doesn't seem very important, does it? That's because you haven't expanded the concept. If you can do that on that level, there's nothing, absolutely nothing, saying it can't be applied on a larger and larger scale. Which is apparently what Zahn managed to do. And what he found seems to have broken him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Look. There's some stuff I like in this. Such as the Lovecraft reference(probably a pseudonym, and a clever one at that), and the fact that this is a pretty accurate use of a "memetic" effect(inb4, you can't say saying something is memetic is wrong, sure, sure).

But, by gods, the melodrama. We apparently found out musical composition is predictable. And… that's it, really. I think this could use some more horror meat to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This didn't really grab me. The concept itself is interesting, but as stated above the execution seemed a little lackluster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like all of it except the last note, which is kind of over-the-top. I have no problems with Zahn delivering the stuff to the Foundation. As has been said, the Foundation is far from the only game in town, and knowledge of them and the supernatural is somewhat widespread. Even if you don't think he has contacts with a GoI or major government, he could still be a Foundation researcher himself doing some stuff on the side.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would argue that if an unaided human ear cannot tell the difference between two frequencies, then they are not distinct for these purposes. So while the audible range is technically infinitely divisible, it is practically limited to a finite number of discernible tones.

By the same token, while variable length does greatly magnify the number of possible combinations, it does not make for an infinite number of human detectable combinations. Because any repetition of previous used patterns will be heard by a human listener as functionally the same. Take the case of Vanilla Ice versus Queen. The beat from "Ice, Ice Baby" is heard as the same as the beat from "Under Pressure", regardless of Vanilla's technically accurate arguments that his beat is distinct because he added an extra snare tap at the end.

So, yes, HUUUUUUGE number of possible songs, an inconceivably large number. But still a finite number. Inherent in the physicality of the human ear and brain is the potential for every song possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is…interesting. Somewhat understated, but still enjoyable by itself. Have my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, that's not accurate. The human ear is only capable of hearing a finite range, but there's an infinite number of tones/frequencies within that range, even if we'd have difficulty discriminating between extremely close ones. And even with a finite number of "discernable" tones, the number of combinations is infinite, because there's no limit on how long the composition is. Add in things like chords, harmonies, variable relative volumes, sustained tone lengths, and effects like deliberate static or fuzzing overlays, and the number of potential sound combinations is literally larger than the number of subatomic particles in the universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. Delightfully eerie, not overstated, and giving me enough of a carrot to chew on for a while.

+1. Good on ya.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spider Robinson brought up a semi-related point in his short story "Melancholy Elephants". To oversimplify, the human ear is only capable of distinguishing a finite number of sounds and those sounds can only be arranged in a finite number of ways. So there is a strict limit on how many songs are possible. To be sure, the set of all possible tunes is a HUGE number; but it's not infinite. That's not even considering that a great many of those arrangements would be a cacophony, not something anyone would interpret as music.

Maybe what Erich has found is the formula that maps the position patterns of "true music" (the arrangements that a human brain considers non-cacophony) among that total set.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation is nigh unreachable. That nigh bit is important. Anyone with the sufficient drive and resources can probably do something like this. After all, we're also not the only game in town. +1 for Eric Zahn in general and apparently having tapped into something approaching the 'The Red'/Life Web from Morrison's Animal Man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I concur about doorstepping in the general case. "Found by Agent Blackbox while on vacation" is the only lazy author cop-out I find more annoying.

But in this specific case I don't think it really applies. Because Erich Zahn is the opposite of that sort of lack-of-story. He is a fully plausible character with a clear and consistent motivation that dictates everything he has done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree. To me, this is like leaving a baby on the doorstep of a convent. "Erich Zann" made these "babies" but can't raise them properly, so he is giving them to the Foundation to analyze with their superior resources.

Plus I like/am unnerved by the idea that music, and by extension, our future is predestined. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no intention of ever using this character again, but I understand the sentiments here. I'll see what I can do about the recovery method.

I also changed the last two notes slightly to tone down the melodrama.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Stuff that just shows up on the Foundation's doorstep with no explanation" is always a silly origin story, and this seems like a hamfisted attempt to create a recurring character.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I believe have successfully predicted the future. Our future. Please respond immediately.

:|
I didn't really feel anything from this, and the anomaly used to host the narrative is pretty boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This concept shows some promise, but the current execution doesn't sell it much- the mystery 3rd party freaking out over predetermined music is all we really get, and it's not interesting enough to counteract the melodramma.
